mnemonics.title: IF-ELSE Decision Path
mnemonics.content: Imagine a fork in the road: IF you take the left path (condition true), you go to the 'then' block; ELSE you take the right path (condition false) and follow the 'else' block.

mnemonics.title: Switch Case Car Gear
mnemonics.content: Think of a car's gear stick: each gear (case) moves the car at a different speed; you select (switch) the gear based on the situation, and the car executes that case's action.

mnemonics.title: For Loop Lap Counter
mnemonics.content: FOR is like a lap counter in a race: you know the number of laps (initialization), each lap you check if you've finished (condition), and then you increment the lap (update).

mnemonics.title: While Loop Condition Check
mnemonics.content: WHILE is like checking a timer: as long as the timer is running (condition true), you keep waiting; once it stops (condition false), you exit.

mnemonics.title: Do-While Postman Delivery
mnemonics.content: DO-WHILE is like a postman delivering mail: they always deliver at least one item (do block) first, then check if there's more mail to deliver (while condition).

mnemonics.title: Break and Continue Traffic Lights
mnemonics.content: BREAK is like a red light that stops the loop immediately; CONTINUE is like a yellow light that skips the current iteration but proceeds to the next.

acronyms.usage: Used when you want to execute a block of code only if a certain condition is true.

acronyms.expansion: Executed when IF condition is false
acronyms.usage: Used after IF to define an alternative block of code when the condition is false.

acronyms.expansion: Select one among multiple cases based on value
acronyms.usage: Used when you have multiple discrete values to compare against a single variable.

acronyms.expansion: A specific value to match in a switch
acronyms.usage: Each CASE represents a possible value of the switch expression.
Used when the number of iterations is known beforehand.
Used when the number of iterations is not known and depends on a condition.

acronyms.expansion: Execute block at least once before checking condition
acronyms.usage: Used when you need the loop body to run at least once.
Used to terminate a loop prematurely based on a condition.

acronyms.expansion: Skip current iteration and move to next
acronyms.usage: Used to skip the rest of the current loop iteration without exiting the loop.

visualTips.visualization: Picture a binary tree: the condition is the root node, the true branch leads to the 'if' block, and the false branch leads to the 'else' block.
Imagine a multi-way rotary switch where each position (case) connects to a different output; the variable selects the position.
Visualize a horizontal timeline with three checkpoints: start (initialization), check (condition), and step (update), looping back until the end.
Think of a revolving door: it keeps turning (repeating) as long as there's power (condition true); when power stops, the door stops.
Picture a stamp machine: it stamps once (do) first, then checks if more stamps are needed (while condition).
Imagine a marathon runner: BREAK is like crossing the finish line to stop the race; CONTINUE is like skipping a water station but continuing the race.

analogies.analogy: Like deciding whether to carry an umbrella: IF it is raining, take the umbrella; ELSE leave it at home.
Like selecting a menu item at a restaurant: you choose (switch) a dish (case), and the chef prepares that specific dish.
Like reading a book for a fixed number of pages each day: you start on page 1, read a set number of pages, and repeat until you finish the book.
Like waiting for a bus: you wait (loop) as long as the bus hasn't arrived (condition true); once it arrives, you stop waiting.
Like taking a taste test: you taste the food (do) at least once, then decide if you need more (while condition).
Like hitting the emergency stop button on a machine to halt operation immediately.
Like skipping a song in a playlist: you stop the current song and move to the next one without stopping the playlist.
